baseline_dominance.justification: Construction identifies that BBS+ Mode B cannot produce an AS-opaque persistent pseudonym (Theorem 4.6, three cases all yield Adv=1/2). The scopeBlindingSecret is a genuine structural advance over Mode B. However, auth0_pm Attack 2 shows Mode B has zero market share — the actual deployed baseline is short-TTL aud-restricted tokens (Mode C), which the construction never benchmarks against. rfc7662_advocate Attack 4 shows RFC 8693 token exchange with per-RS scoped issuance + PPID is a distinct baseline with a different trust topology the construction doesn't engage. The construction beats a theoretical best-case baseline but not the operational one buyers will compare against.

formal_security_argument.justification: IND-UNL-AS game is explicitly defined with adversarial AS as the threat actor. Theorem 4.6 formalizes BBS+ Mode B failure across three cases. Corollary 4.7 summary table exists. However: (1) cryptographer Attack 1 is devastating — the construction never specifies the full public-input vector, so if the credential commitment C is a public input (as it almost certainly must be for RS verification), the nullifier is unlinkable but C trivially re-links all presentations, making the theorem vacuously correct. (2) No setup trust assumption stated (cryptographer Attack 2). (3) Nonce oracle not modeled in the game (cryptographer Attack 3, rfc7662_advocate Attack 3). (4) No entropy lower bound on scopeBlindingSecret (cryptographer Attack 4). Threat model exists but critical modeling gaps and no reduction sketch connecting scopeBlindingSecret to a named hardness assumption.

implementability.justification: scopeBlindingSecret + Poseidon hash maps to existing Bolyra primitives (AgentPolicy circuit, BabyJubjub EdDSA, nullifier scheme). Dual Groth16/PLONK build already exists. However: (1) no circuit cost stated — no constraint count or proving time target for the scope-blinded variant. (2) cryptographer Attack 1 implies fixing the public-input linkage may require a second ZK layer (recursive proof or split-proof design) to hide credential commitment, which is a new primitive outside Phase 1 budget. (3) auth0_pm Attack 4 shows 15s Groth16 proof time fails multi-RS MCP tool-call scenarios (3-7 hops per turn = 45-105s latency). No proof-caching design that avoids leaking scope pre-selection.

adversarial_survival.justification: Tier 3 surfaced multiple fatal attacks the construction cannot answer. cryptographer Attack 1 (credential commitment as stable cross-RS public input) is structurally devastating — the scopeBlindingSecret blinds the nullifier but not the credential commitment, and the construction never specifies that C is hidden. auth0_pm Attack 1 (CU-as-AS has BSA/AML duty to surveil the exact graph being hidden) makes the primary scenario self-defeating. cu_ciso Attack 4 (unlinkability blinds the CU while leaving agent operator with full correlation) shows the privacy runs in the wrong direction. spiffe_engineer Attack 2 (TLS SVID re-links at transport layer) bypasses the ZK layer entirely. Of 20 attacks across 5 personas, 17 are marked 'No — construction must address' and 3 'Partially.' No persona failed to find an unanswered attack.

scenario_fit.justification: Two concrete scenarios named: cross-CU member agent (CU-as-AS must not see merchant graph) and healthcare delegation across providers. Both name stakeholders (CU CISO, healthcare provider). However: auth0_pm Attack 1 shows the CU scenario is self-defeating — under BSA/AML, OFAC, and SAR obligations the CU has a legal duty to surveil exactly the graph being hidden. cu_ciso Attack 2 confirms this: unlinkability from AS means unlinkability from the CU's own compliance monitoring, violating NCUA Part 748 and GLBA Safeguards. cu_ciso Attack 4 shows the construction blinds the regulated entity while leaving the unregulated agent operator with full correlation capability — the opposite of what a CISO needs. The property is a regulatory liability, not load-bearing, in the named deployment.

gaps: FATAL: Specify full public-input vector of scope-blinded proof; demonstrate no deterministic per-credential identifier (e.g., credential commitment C) appears as a public input across scope presentations. Without this, unlinkability is vacuously correct. May require per-scope commitment C_i = H(C, scope_i) or rerandomizable commitment scheme.

Add Mode C baseline (short-TTL aud-restricted tokens à la WorkOS/Auth0/Cloudflare Access) to Section 3 and formally show why AS still observes refresh cadence per RS, leaking the traffic graph. Also run explicit Bolyra-vs-RFC 8693 comparison for delegation.

Restructure CU scenario: separate (a) CU's internal compliance function (must retain audit capability per BSA/AML/NCUA Part 748) from (b) cross-CU or merchant-to-merchant correlation. The scopeBlindingSecret may still win for inter-CU unlinkability but the current framing conflates the regulated CU's own audit needs with the privacy guarantee.

Define adversary model for malicious agent operator who holds both credential key and scopeBlindingSecret. Current game blinds the AS/issuer but the agent operator has full correlation capability — wrong direction for CU CISO who must oversee third-party vendors under GLBA §314.4(f).

Extend IND-UNL-AS game to model: (a) Issue(W) oracle so adversary has issuance log, (b) nonce oracle interactions (who generates sessionNonce and what metadata AS retains), (c) multi-epoch credential rotation with scopeBlindingSecret lifecycle specified (per-epoch vs persistent).

State setup trust assumption explicitly: either commit to public MPC ceremony with N≥2 independent contributors for Groth16, or argue unlinkability under PLONK universal SRS. Current construction has no setup trust statement.

Specify scopeBlindingSecret entropy requirement (SBS ← {0,1}^λ, uniform, secure random, never transmitted) and lifecycle (storage, rotation, revocation, loss recovery). Address SOC 2 Type II audit story and emergency revocation under unlinkability constraints.

Address transport-layer correlation: TLS mTLS SVID, timing side channels (±ms server logs), and SPIFFE bundle federation metadata all re-link at layers below the ZK proof. Specify required deployment topology (proxy, jitter, anonymization) or bound the residual leakage.
